Name: 8_teaching_class_semester_students
Representative_Docs: ['i ve always been weary about teacher burnout and feel deeply called to helping people so i ve been extremely mindful of taking all the preventative actions to stop burnout i try not to work weekends unless absolutely necessary i leave campus on breaks when i can i close my room to students for alone time i do breathing exercises focus on positives and i am so defeated i teach english for high school and love the books i teach and love teaching to students i love sectioning out information having patience for apathetic learners and put so much love into my lessons i was a school avoidant kid before covid coined the term i have aces and a double degree in psychology on top of english education but i can t help but think it s not good for me to be in this career i m an extrovert and a people person but the students need so much i m constantly overstimulated and lately i m walking into work already at and coming home below empty my district takes an iep for absolutely anything one meeting a goal was for a student to get a boyfriend because she s struggled socially i can t enforce a late work policy or a cellphone policy because it s not district wide and any teacher that has tried becomes so hated by the students they give up feeling little support to fight the fight i don t need the kids to like me by any means but every single year only year to be fair but nonetheless not only have i had students that hate me but they viscerally hate me i ve been pushed physically by students been issued death threats a standing applause from a class when a student went on a tirade that it s my fault for students using ai when i ve encouraged ai for idea mapping per professional developments telling me to do as much my department has mean girls that actively create a hostile work environment gossip and won t even respond to hello s in the hallway i ve been sexually harassed to the point of having to report because other older teachers stepped in and said i had to i ve been given different love letters of confession from male coworkers despite having a partner my whole time working there that i rave about to all last month i went to a guidance counselor for help and told her that the student in question is one of the most disrespectful i ve had in my years at that school and she said she respectfully disagrees only to follow with that she s very close with that family and knows he s a good kid today a case manager said i was wildly inappropriate in a parent meeting for telling a student that other staff members have also reported to me that she cheats and i was then kicked me out of the room i tried to explain i was by no means targeting her but solely confronting a real problem and i want to empower the student only to be cut off and told more firmly to leave the room i have to teach in said student s class tomorrow and i just don t have it in me i had to leave work because i couldn t get myself to stop crying the meeting discussed how academics aren t everything and how i can t stop this student from shining because english isn t her strong suit but isn t that quite literally my job as an academic professional i don t have it in me to fight the fight for kids to learn how to cheat better or convince a counselor to pass them despite not doing anything i don t have it in me to be the only person in the room bringing enthusiasm and trying so hard for little to no appreciation i m tired of being friends with the students or not considering their personal situations enough i m tired of being too easy of a class and not challenging kids while also being too hard on the kids and assigning too much i m tired of caring more than the kids and being hated for it i m tired of catty coworkers excluding gossiping and actively targeting i love teaching so much i love the kids that i ve helped and the beautiful letters of gratitude i ve gotten i love the staff that supports me and i love teaching on the good days but the bad days are just so bad and overpowering the good by far maybe it s just june or maybe it was just a really shitty class of students this year but i m so deeply considering switching careers i m just so defeated and sad', 'last fall term was my first back after covid lay offs i was so excited and immediately so deflated and then walked on eggshells all year one of my classes was international students who all used ai and when i reported it to their program lead and my dean i was the problem and didn t get classes at that college again i was new at that school and oh well the classes i had at the other college the one i had taught at previously were the last minute sign ups add on classes which typically are wonky one class was great and one was full of as my dean called it the covid crap we re still putting up with that class took me down in evals the worst i d gotten in my year career i was shaken and i continued to get the add on classes all year my status that of the least senior adjunct i asked for support and maybe made a poor showing in doing so there were some wins for sure and students who told me over and over again best professor at this school i wouldn t have stayed in college if you wouldn t have been my first professor and other such swoon during the year last year i sat long and hard and spent a lot of time reading on this reddit which made me know i wasn t alone thank you and came to the realization that quitting wasn t what i wanted to do adjuncting is fucking hard emotionally it triggers all that stress stuff we are told to avoid and it is a ridiculous and demoralizing professional model especially when new graduates are regularly hired into permanent advising positions while we aren t but teaching is what i love maybe that s what makes it so hard i got a good tip from our program lead who explained courses fill by order on the list of choices for this fall term i picked the classes listed at the top both filled within two days last spring and wow the difference yes both are mostly full of high school students now in a duel credit program who were th graders last june and i have had to give my high school habits don t cut it here speech to all four of the classes or maybe we re just done with that covid crap this week their first essays came in and i have been dazzled amazed engrossed and enlightened i also adopted the let them theory sounds easy but it s hard i have to write myself little notes like in this instance yesterday was a reading seminar day in one of my classes several students didn t listen to the high school habits don t cut it here speech and missed the deadline for an essay they were clearly still pouting when i walked in for the seminar i set the timer for the first round and no one spoke i let them i let them sit there and pout i calmly wrote in my writer s notebook after about four minutes i noticed others those not in that round of speaking do the same the past months have been the hardest of my year career yes because of all the systemic weakness we all grapple with and post about yes because students are lazy i wrote my first behavior reports ever last year i got my first grade complaints ever last year i got my first ever student complaints last year directly to my dean i got my first bad evals last year i had a horrible pass rate i think the biggest hit for me was how mean and manipulative the non doers are i ve also had to have really direct tough not mean but not just giving the nice face and hoping for the best conversations with students around boundaries acceptable behavior and student habits how to talk to me what college is and isn t the behavior that brings these conversations seems to be the norm more than the exception these days it has become habit to say college doesn t work like that when a student tries to operate from a blame perspective turns in excuses instead of work comes unprepared all the things just thought i d share', 'i am a tenure track assistant professor at a non us research intensive university i am mainly evaluated on my research impact i saw teaching an undergraduate course as a way of motivating the students to master technically challenging concepts as well as identifying keen students who might be interested in doing research i have thus taken on more teaching responsibilities than what is required of me but i am starting to regret it my university does not ban the use of ai for students when chatgpt first came out the provost intially banned it but he quickly backtracked due to complaints from the students and some faculty members last semester was when i first notice our undergraduate students use ai on the assignments i was wondering why the students were getting the same question wrong and made the same mistakes i fed my assignment into a llm and knew why i felt really silly for putting more effort grading the assignments than the students put into doing my assignments this semester i am teaching a course on introductory statistics this was a new course created especially for students in our undergraduate program because our students took the introductory statistics course from the department of statistics and more than half failed the course i looked at the teaching material from the other statistics course and i saw that the material was really high quality the lecture notes were immaculuate and the assignments were well designed and assessed foundamental skills if this course was available when i was a undergrad i would have taken it however the students hated the course bemoaned about the workload and the students even filed a complaint letter with the dean regarding the instructor for this course so for my new replacment course for introductory statistics i added more programming component but kept the mathematical derivation component i also reduced the weighting of the exam as i catch up on my assignment marking i am just starting to realize how pervasive the use of ai has become at the undergraduate level at our university in class i had repeatedly discouraged students to use ai on the assignments and i encouraged them to use ai for revising english and providing second opinions i fed my assignment into a popular llm and discussed why the outputs are bad some of the students who did attend my lectures submitted the very same code that i had pointed out was bad two students submited the exact same ai generated buggy code when i finish going through the assignments i am sure that i will identify many other duplicate submissions from other students i will have to hand out a lot of zeros one of the students was pretty keen and asked lots of questions relatively speaking i had also created an online multiple choice quiz question in which i used ai geneated answers in the available options only one student got this quiz question right most students selected the option that matched the ai generated answer even though i had walked through the mathematical derivation step by step in class for this very problem in class a few days before the quiz it s really now difficult to design good math and programming questions that are appropriate for the undergraduate level but that ai cannot solve the students don t want to grind through basic problem sets to develop foundamental mathematical and programming skills the students use ai to solve these problem sets when i create more challenging real world assignment problems students become confused because they never learned the fundamental skills from the basic problem sets and they just give me ai generated nonsense to grade so in the era of ai at a university that does not ban the use of ai for students how can i motivate the students to go through the pain of solving technical challenging math and programming questions many many years ago i took geoffrey hinton s undergraduate course on neural network as far as i can tell he used this course as a way of identifying keen students and he holds office hours to engage with them his course was actually fairly easy we were expected to master the technical details the assignments consisted of playing with pre implemented machine learning models and seeing how they behave in response to tuning parameter and training parameter changes the exams only tested superficial high level knowledge of machine learning my favourite undergraduate course however was on algorithm and design this was the most challenging course that i have ever taken i was a biology major i actually failed the first mid term exam but got an a in the course in the end i loved the challenging assignments and i had to work with other students on them and attend office hours to even have a chance at solving the problems if these two courses were taught today the assignments in these two courses would be ineffective since the students would just ai the instructors would have had to go out of their way to make the assignments difficult for ai to solve which would likely make the assignments too difficult for keen students who do not rely on ai i know that prompt injection can work at least until the students realize what s happening my colleague used this technique on his quiz question in the same course and the vast majority of our students failed his question the students simply didn t bother to read the prompt that they copied and pasted even though my colleague explicitly warned them about reading the pasted prompt carefully in class my colleague and i are now pretty sure that most of the students will fail the exam i had warn the students that i won t curve the grades but i am guessing that this warning will do about as much good as telling a pack a day smoker about the health risks of smoking']

Name: 9_writing_ai writing_write_students
Representative_Docs: ['this might be a sign to pay attention or a sign they re not giving ai the rigor it deserves look i have no issues with conceding the overreaching point that writing makes you a more critical thinker and to use your admittedly simplistic word smarter i agree about that but what is it about the process of writing that does that where is it effective and where isn t it effective i think we d both agree that copying hemingway s a farewell to arms word for word doesn t necessarily do anything to make a student smarter just to give an extreme example but writing about hemingway s a farewell to arms about the language chosen and why about the themes that are there the themes that aren t there that could be it s relationship to hemingway the actual man and his personal history and using direct quotes from a farwell to arms to emphasis your points those could make a student smarter both are writing but i think we d both agree that each are significantly different from the other in terms of what we d call writing which leads to the next step is there any portion of that process however we define writing in the make students smarter sense that ai can enhance where does it detract why does it detract what is it supplanting i think we have to get our heads around that level of detail to understand how do you guide students about ai i think this makes morons is just too simplistic and honestly doesn t pass the smell test it rings inauthentic and doesn t match real lived experience i can ask ai to help me make a syllabus outline and hey it does a pretty good job i learned about a few things that other people are discussing in their courses on that subject matter and some of them i think i should include but there s others that i don t for a variety of reasons it also confirmed the broad strokes of what i was thinking should be included or excluded i re worded what it did to give it a little more style and to flow the way i wanted but essentially i used ai to write my syllabus is that bad writing or is that a good use of the technology personally i think ai helped me and that s not a bad thing but if it continues to run unchecked through the universities it will more importantly mentally cripple an entire generation which is why i remain perplexed by humanities and some social science as well disengagement unchecked implies a degree of engagement ignoring the problem doesn t help banning the problem doesn t help especially for teenagers who are notorious for following all the rules to a t sarcasm in case it wasn t obvious i think we have to be more upfront with our students and explain to them what ai can and cannot do for them which means we have to know what ai can and cannot do for them beyond ai bad', 'i have moved from being mad at mla and other professional organizations to being disheartened at how fast they all capitulated to ai in higher education or any level of education all the published practices for utilizing ai in writing instruction at least everything i have read and i have tried my best to keep up are based upon anecdotal evidence not data or longitudinal studies etc in fact on the contrary the most notable studies about ai s impact on students from microsoft mit and columbia all of which have been discussed ad nauseum on this sub point out the major drawbacks and warnings for educators of course the conclusions can be field dependent but i m concerned with reading development of critical thinking skills and writing we hear about how ai can be used for brainstorming yet the published research indicates that students who use ai for brainstorming are more likely to be influenced by the ai output so much that they will tailor their work to that output in addition the whole misnomer of artificial intelligence is being used as marketing tool to students because as we all know there is nothing intelligent about an llm it s no more than predictive model of words based on the algorithm that was trained on a particular data set of written text in fact my spouse who works in a related field in academia just attended a conference session yesterday about the llm called novelai if you aren t familiar with it its intended usage is for creative writing and images at any rate the presenter a linguist who also has a background in computer science took the attendees under the hood of the llm if you will to illustrate how it was trained how it works how it strings together words based on tokens to write its text and so on my spouse came away so very disillusioned with novelai one of the largest data sets it was trained on by the way are posts from reddit the presenter pointed out many biases the llm demonstrated argh we are all doomed so it seems to me we have moved so unfortunately fast as a collective to surrender our creativity our autonomy our voices to this technology i try to tell my students that many of them would not have had the ability to read or write if they were born just generations ago and many of them in fact would have been forcefully prevented from doing so some get it a lot just don t care i should wrap this up the only things that keep me going are the small wins this morning i got an email from my hs child s english teacher announcing that they will be reading dracula over the next few weeks this is an advanced class in a good school in a good well funded district but they don t use textbooks everything is online and they never read books in print especially whole books not just excepts of course the teacher was announcing where parents could purchase the book for their child but i was just so very happy that my kid will be reading a class in print that i now have plan to take her to a brick and mortar bookstore this weekend what i mean to get to is i think in the future there will be two worlds the reading writing and thinking class and the techno class that seems very dystopian but oh well', 'tl dr i teach first year writing so i ll give you some insight on what i think about the subject as a whole which leads to what i think you are encountering ai like a hammer when used as a tool can be useful but students often incorrectly assume that with ai the end result is a nicely written document or even that a properly written document is what i am asking of them when students rely on ai to help with their basic writing because they are scrambling they wasted time think it sounds better etc the outcome tends to just be plagiarism in their case one cannot just skirt around the fact that writing a research paper is a process that has to be developed in recursive steps moving one direction revisiting something and moving forward but differently as originally intended as a writing professor i encourage students to type back and forth with ai ask questions and to use the tool to help them understand something they need clarification on sometimes someone else can explain the topic in a way they better understand i might not be explaining it correctly or in a way that they are connecting with the concept if they are writing an argumentative paper a student may need a helping push in order to understand the other side different view point getting out of their head to understand the other side of the argument empathy is sometimes very hard when the side they have chosen is oversaturated in content if they are tasked with finding resources of a different view point the content of that resource may be difficult to understand ai might summarize it better i am happy to work with students are their specifics but they don t always ask and i don t know what i don t know unless they tell me what the issues are they are encountering i tend to explain that ai should be treated as another form of social media in that it s a collective think tank that sometimes gets it right sometimes it gets it wrong and most likely is oversaturated with content that gets pushed to you even if you didn t ask for it if a student is using social media ai to help in the research process they still have to cite the source a super off the cuff in text citation then looks like this according to ai we can assume the general understanding of this chapter to be x y and z chatgpt in this regard ai best works i have found if you input the quote you are trying to understand fully and then ask ai for a summary you will still want to read your resource because again ai can get it wrong but if you need a better summary ask ai to summarize i tell students that it then just becomes easier to put the literature into their own personal portable algorithm brain because writing is a process and ai at this point can t teach you the process it may be able to summarize the process for you and give you tailored step by step instructions on how to write a research paper but the process of actually writing the paper is only something that you learn by incorporating multiple tools the same way that you can t build a house with just a hammer you need all the other tools too it sounds like you are finding a current limitations to ai the first limitation might be that the content you need to access is behind the copyright law and so a second limitation is that ai does not often encounter what you are inputting and still requires more base line data to then turn out your content in the correct format you will have to scaffold outwardly like i m talking about above separate to ai when you approach ai you may need to approach it as if you have to teaching it what you need before it can turn out what you are asking i think ai was conceptualized by scientists and the programming will not inherently include your field in the mix unless you put it in i think this might be your solution for now the trick might be to keep doing your back and forth with it and use it as a tool without assuming that it can automatically turn out what you need it to just yet but i could be wrong or i might be missing something so feel free any one chime in i m not an ai expert in any capacity but i do know a thing or two about english and language usage and i often find where ai misses the mark with language and style thoughts']

Name: 10_juniors_junior_senior_seniors
Representative_Docs: ['i ve said this quite a few times now but i ll say it again when you read these articles they all imply that companies are hiring juniors to do work using their knowledge and now this work is being replaced by ai no software company is hiring juniors for manpower you hire them as an investment because some of them will stay after upskilling with objectively lower salaries than if you had hired a senior from the get go you also allow your mid and early senior developers to mentor someone which is a very important skill which simply can not be progressed without a mentee hiring juniors also often helps you identify flaws in your onboarding process and documentation or general issues with you developer experience as jaded yoe senior dev will just power through whatever absurd local setup everyone on the team has by now gotten desensitized to i ve never been on a project and said damn wish we had some juniors so we could finish this faster if any other senior developer has been in this situation feel free to correct me but i highly doubt it so whenever you read articles like this think to yourself was this company previously relying on junior developers to make progress on its product hint probably not', 'yes but if you are a business owner why would you hire junior devs at this point and i get that as an industry if you don t hire and train junior devs now you won t have senior devs in the future but as someone that is seeking to profit maximize why not augment your senior staff with ai agents why hire juniors at all', 'where do you think seniors come from companies will always need to train juniors because today s juniors are tomorrow s seniors and senior engineers will always be in demand when new tooling arrives it can lower the bar for what a company needs to hire for but it doesn t eliminate the need for people business owners hire to delegate work they don t suddenly decide to do everything themselves just because the tools got better actually what it can do is let them hire a less experienced and cheaper person because the tooling compensates for some skill gaps which in cases might actually increase junior positions you might argue that seniors no longer need to delegate to juniors because they can just use ai but honestly seniors were never delegating directly to juniors in the first place juniors get trained into mid level engineers and those mid levels take on a lot of the legwork juniors are still needed to fill that pipeline and ai is still a long way from replacing a solid mid level engineer because there is a lot more to the job than coding also no intelligent business owner is going to rely solely on ai to build and maintain a product when there are financial and legal consequences for getting it wrong they ll still want qualified people in the loop for those people to exist companies will need to invest in training juniors i don t see junior positions disappearing i do see competition and entry barrier increasing']

Name: 11_kids_children_child_parents
Representative_Docs: ['at that age they tend to start switching to quantity over quality everything rushes everything seems important they also start having competing interests it can paralyze a kid it can also teach kids the just get it done philosophy if you slow things way down you are solidifying that quality is important and to pay attention you have a classical philosophy i had one too as they get older their ideas multiply too they start comparing ideas you say vs what youtube says vs a friend vs whoever this leads to them wanting to report it all so nothing gets left out they are not yet ready to discern their own opinion well why it s their opinion based upon facts so they have collected so much information and don t know what to do with it so this also paralyzes them if you ask tell me the best way to they will freeze or give several ways oral recitation is so important at this stage more so today with ai oral recitation is a memory tool it s a humanity tool it s a different part of the brain being worked for discerning things a fun thing to do at this age is have a child silently read a meaty article full of opinions and data ask them what they thought and they will stammer regurgitate and pause have a child that age verbally read that same article and they will give you data and their opinions and add in some questions oral reading is how that discernment part of the brain best develops think how an adult reacts if you read a verbal statement that you disagree with you just move on but how hard is it to verbally state an opinion you disagree with without saying your opinion or qualifying things or you hear someone talking and you notice faster if it may not be truth with ai development and writing ai has not learned the humanity component if you read something orally you can easily detect ai generated writing if you read it you won t it s flow that humans naturally do that we can detect truth originality confidence etc this is why you always should read anything you write for publication out loud and why things like im typing now may read strange i m not going back and reading it out loud before replying but emphasis on quality yes this is where you truly want one strong sentence over weak ones copywork should end around the age of if you re looking at development before that it is an excellent training tool for memorizing and learning and developing brain pathways for understanding in fact schools use to all have chalkboards and copy work every day they stopped it when electronics and copy machines came into schools that is a key factor in reduced learning', 'i share this fear as you say this is a new breed of kid we are at the beginning in high school i guess middle school teachers already got hit with this of a wave of kids who are fundamentally different than we are they have grown up in a virtual reality they are not wired the same way that we are instead of learning social behaviors from parents and unplugged kids they have learned behavior from the internet which intrinsically rewards self centered and outlandish behavior their brains are hard wired differently too the dopamine cycle is well known now and the immersion in the internet world has created this apathy and their short attention spans and right now i m seeing short ads for microsoft s ai and they are selling it as a means to be more productive the truth is that these kids are producing products essays study guides etc but they are circumventing the process i e they are taking shortcuts they think the product is what matters in education and now they are becoming widely and deeply bored since products are available in seconds and here s ms telling them ai is a great tool they showed a young woman wearing many hats as requests for this and that came from here co workers and so she sits down opens ai and produces the talking points somebody needs for a presentation except here s the thing why on earth would anybody hire a person whose only skill is to ask ai for something literally anybody can do that and there is no need to hire somebody to do it for you so where are the jobs going to be for short attention span apathetic graduates who look for what is interesting rather than what is important and have never exercised their critical thinking skills to any extent these kids are screwed next year i am starting every class i teach english with a brief unit that tries to tell kids that ai may help them get a passing grade but it will undermine their ability to live a productive life but i m afraid it will have the same effect as telling them to eat their vegetables', 'this is a mistake children do not need to be taught how to use ai ai is the most intuitive technology that humankind has ever invented our leaders once thought that students were digital natives and didn t need to learn to use the computer because they ve been pacified by a tablet since the age of two so they cut the computer classes tablets and phones are not the same as pcs their operating systems are designed to be intuitive easy to pick up easy to use the skills do not translate well to operating systems like windows macintosh or linux consequently as a millennial teacher i work with students who are as bad with technology as my something year old parents i mention this to caution against making the same mistake with ai ai is an incredibly intuitive piece of technology if you need to instruct it all you need to do is interact with it as if you were interacting with another person any changes you need to make to its output you can directly say hey i like these parts but i want to focus on this part here s how we re going to change it this is not something that needs to be taught to people who know how to interact with other people have critical thinking skills and are able to work through problems people who are able to read and synthesize information however that s where we re failing these kids they are increasingly unable to read at grade level they have so little endurance that they struggle to get through short reading passages they struggle to answer questions that require sustained thought they want everything spoon fed to them as a teacher i m living this every day and it is alarming if we think teaching these children to rely on yet more technology to think and do for them is in their best interest then there is something gravely wrong with our society reliance on these technologies dulls the mind they will create a generation of ignorant and easily manipulated people people that we re supposed to rely on to solve some of the greatest problems our species has ever known']

Name: 12_cheating_cheat_students_students cheating
Representative_Docs: ['yes i am not a teacher i am a current grad student i will say from the student s perspective that cheatings is significantly more widespread and common than teachers think it is it happens very frequently and it is very easy to get away with the amount of students who are suspected of or caught cheating by teachers are just the one s who were sloppy at hiding it the true number of students who cheat is much higher cheating is no longer something that is only done by lazy students and students that aren t smart enough to learn the material properly it is done by everyone especially the high performers in my experience cheating is even more common amongst the straight a students because their ability to cheat gets them the a s in the first place the difference between them and the lower gpa students that get caught more often is that the straight a students are better at covering up the evidence so they get away with it student s who don t cheat at least some of the time are the minority to be clear this is not just due to ai this has been the case for years prior to the release of chatgpt chegg was the go to for students trying to cheat it was so popular that paid subscription sites for assignment answers start becoming its own industry from other companies trying to replicate chegg s business model until ai came around and replaced it', 'i get the argument students have been cheating since time immemorial to be sure however no student is obliged to be dishonest in their ai use just like no student is obliged to cheat peers have always seen their colleagues in class cheating they ve always heard about students cheating but they chose not to cheat because of potential penalties and to maintain their moral compass not even for the altruistic goal of actually learning something i do see a sliver of students using ai and i call them out i haven t had anyone really go to the mat in defending their use of ai while insisting that they didn t at least not yet i suspect that is the next thing i am going to see since i read so many accounts of precisely that here on this subreddit however aversion is still a reasonable course to plot university administrators however have spent the last several decades supporting the notion that education is a consumer not a public good that is purchased by the student for their own use as they see fit that is probably why i ve read so many accounts of universities now putting the burden on faculty or simply embracing which means different things to different institutions the use of ai here s the rub for me ai can already complete traditional assignments better than most people i couldn t disagree more quicker absolutely using reasonable logic to construct answers yeah easier to cheat you betcha but better than most people nah ai output is vanilla non creative garbage that reads like a wikipedia entry it s not that difficult to id i know the online ai checkers aren t perfect but they re pretty good universities should see this as an existential threat to their existence rather than another technology that they must embrace because students are embracing it this is kinda like a public university calling for an end to governmental financial aid basically punching themselves in the face because it might be entertaining for their customers and earn them a few more tuition dollars i ve never stopped using pencil and paper scantron exams in class for my introductory courses in my upper division courses exams are blue book and handwritten for the larger written work i ve only had a couple of instances where majors had the temerity to present ai output as their own work i suspect the student population will get bolder but instructors will still be able to id ai output in fact i think we ll get better at it going forward the bottom line is that if an instructor prohibits ai use and the student feels like being dishonest is mandatory then that s on the student we cannot reasonably be held responsible for student dishonesty', 'when i was first entering academia the general mindset was that cheating was a symptom of bad teaching that the average student cheated because they felt overwhelmed and unsupported sure there were a few genuinely lazy students but most cheating could be addressed through better instruction i ve met similar comments outside of academia too i think ai has absolutely disrupted this viewpoint in the past cheating was pretty easy to prove if you could be caught especially with software like turnitin now students feel like ai can t be proven they think they can t actually be punished for cheating so we re seeing cheating everywhere while there are several other circumstances influences this i think this is the primary difference in the numbers of cheaters i saw pre and post chatgpt the gap in this time too small to be caused only by social differences i often wonder how this has impacted our general understanding of cheating i think a lot of the old philosophy failed to take into account the perceived risks of cheating which would have been much higher in the past']

Name: 16_detectors_ai detectors_detector_flagged
Representative_Docs: ['not very accurate honestly most ai detectors turnitin gptzero copyleaks have high false positive rates especially with formal well structured writing they re guessing based on patterns not actual evidence of ai use that s why even human written essays can get flagged they re useful as a rough indicator but they shouldn t be treated as definitive proof the best way to get away from these false flags to to use a good humanizer like walterwrites ai or if you ve time then manually making some small changes in the tone and structure can help', 'ai detectors are often trained to spot patterns of phrasing and structure that resemble ai output as a result they can flag precise logical writing the same way they d flag ai generated text i also have to humanize my stuffs before submitting however i ve found an ai detector that is much reliable you can try proofademic ai it s quite good', 'which detector are you using there was a study recently which found that people who use ai regularly can detect it with over accuracy most of the ai detectors did significantly worse except for pangram which did about as well as human experts it also generalizes to newly released llms for example it had no problem detecting deepseek r when it was first released as well as gpt while detectors such as gptzero generally classified content from new llms as human generated it also apparently does really well with text that was first generated by ai and then rewritten by a person or another ai it also has a pretty low false positive rate about in compared to about in for gptzero']

Name: 17_online_asynchronous_person_courses
Representative_Docs: ['i ve scrapped and am redoing all my assessments in my asynchronous online course after a disastrous experience trying to test students through online quizzes and exams last year after consulting with colleagues at my institution in other departments who teach asynchronous virtual courses they all recommended in person exams they told me that the grades students have going into the in person exams may be inflated but many of those students cannot do well on the in person exam so my colleagues who teach virtual asynchronous courses are requiring in person final exams at my institution classroom space is very tight and without an assigned timeslot for the class having a midterm exam in person is logistically impossible however the university allows for in person final exams for asynchronous courses during the usual end of term exam period university administrators have made it clear that even though it s an online asynchronous course instructors can require some in person elements like the final exam if students live more than km away from campus they can request to write it in a proctored setting at their expense but the vast majority show up on exam day and write it in the same room just like a traditional exam during covid and prior to that for online asynchronous courses my institution used software where students wrote their exams at home while being visually recorded this software had some problematic elements and my university no longer allows it so i had relied on open book timed online assessments and it went as well as you d expect it was a mess i quickly realized that you could just pop the questions into chat gpt in real time and do reasonably well those assessments were useless so my course is now a mix of active learning group assessments infographics and the in person final exam i ve been notifying students when they register that the final exam will be in person and it will be worth of their final grade i m telling them now even though the class doesn t begin until january i explain the details of how to request to write a proctored in person exam where they live they can hire someone at a local college or university to proctor their in person exam so whether students read this email i have notified them about the in person final exam i will mandate if i m allowed that to pass the course they must pass the in person final so a student can t just coast through the online assessments bomb the in person final and still pass i have to check to see if i can attach that must pass condition to the in person final exam some of my colleagues and i are taking full advantage of our institution s policy that in person final exams are allowed for asynchronous courses i ve heard that it s a hassle to physically get students exams when they ve written in their own locations but that in person final exams are the only way to go if academic integrity is important to you', 'let us be honest with ourselves since the rise of generative artificial intelligence especially tools like chatgpt online teaching has become increasingly ineffective for real learning no matter what measures we take whether it is using lockdown browsers designing alternative assessments or increasing monitoring students are still finding ways to cheat the integrity of learning is being lost in many online environments online education may seem convenient not only for instructors but also for students they can attend class from home on their own time and with minimal effort it appears easier and more flexible but as human beings we are naturally drawn to shortcuts we tend to choose the path of least resistance even if it comes at the cost of long term growth many students convince themselves that online education is beneficial but the truth is that they are not learning in a meaningful or lasting way universities are strongly promoting online programs right now most likely because they are increasing short term enrollment however is this really sustainable i have noticed a consistent pattern students in degree programs with mostly or fully online courses tend to struggle many do not stay engaged and a large number eventually drop out before reaching their second or third year but even those who do complete their degrees often do so by relying heavily on generative artificial intelligence and other shortcuts at that point we must ask what is the value of a degree if the student has not actually learned the material what kind of future does that prepare them for another major issue is the lack of opportunities for networking and personal development college is not just about acquiring academic knowledge it is also about building confidence learning how to communicate and forming real human connections these experiences help students develop the soft skills needed to succeed in both professional and personal life online education removes or limits most of these opportunities when students spend more time in front of screens than in real social environments they miss out on learning how to collaborate lead and handle interpersonal challenges to be completely honest part of the problem comes from us as faculty some of us prefer online teaching because it is more comfortable and manageable but what is convenient for instructors is not necessarily better for students and it certainly does not serve the long term interests of higher education if we continue making decisions based on short term benefits we will face serious long term consequences the value of a four year degree will decline our graduates will be less competitive in the job market employers will begin to question the capabilities of students from certain programs more and more students will turn to trade schools or alternative forms of education that provide more practical and reliable outcomes eventually as enrollment drops and public trust erodes universities will begin cutting programs faculty positions will be at risk i understand that online education may be a significant part of the future technology is evolving and flexibility is important but can we at least strike a balance for in person degree programs can we limit online instruction to to percent of the curriculum that way students still receive the value of direct engagement hands on learning and the campus experience making more and more foundational or starter courses entirely online in every program is only going to devalue the degree in the long run we need to ask ourselves are we preserving the mission of education or are we compromising it for the sake of convenience and enrollment numbers', 'let us be honest with ourselves since the rise of generative artificial intelligence especially tools like chatgpt online teaching has become increasingly ineffective for real learning no matter what measures we take whether it is using lockdown browsers designing alternative assessments or increasing monitoring students are still finding ways to cheat the integrity of learning is being lost in many online environments online education may seem convenient not only for instructors but also for students they can attend class from home on their own time and with minimal effort it appears easier and more flexible but as human beings we are naturally drawn to shortcuts we tend to choose the path of least resistance even if it comes at the cost of long term growth many students convince themselves that online education is beneficial but the truth is that they are not learning in a meaningful or lasting way universities are strongly promoting online programs right now most likely because they are increasing short term enrollment however is this really sustainable i have noticed a consistent pattern students in degree programs with mostly or fully online courses tend to struggle many do not stay engaged and a large number eventually drop out before reaching their second or third year but even those who do complete their degrees often do so by relying heavily on generative artificial intelligence and other shortcuts at that point we must ask what is the value of a degree if the student has not actually learned the material what kind of future does that prepare them for another major issue is the lack of opportunities for networking and personal development college is not just about acquiring academic knowledge it is also about building confidence learning how to communicate and forming real human connections these experiences help students develop the soft skills needed to succeed in both professional and personal life online education removes or limits most of these opportunities when students spend more time in front of screens than in real social environments they miss out on learning how to collaborate lead and handle interpersonal challenges to be completely honest part of the problem comes from us as faculty some of us prefer online teaching because it is more comfortable and manageable we are also getting lazy but what is convenient for instructors is not necessarily better for students and it certainly does not serve the long term interests of higher education if we continue making decisions based on short term benefits we will face serious long term consequences the value of a four year degree will decline our graduates will be less competitive in the job market employers will begin to question the capabilities of students from certain programs more and more students will turn to trade schools or alternative forms of education that provide more practical and reliable outcomes eventually as enrollment drops and public trust erodes universities will begin cutting programs faculty positions will be at risk i understand that online education may be a part of the future technology is evolving and flexibility does matter especially for working professionals or students with special needs some courses might work reasonably well online but personally i believe that for stem programs such as engineering nursing and mathematics online education is not effective these fields require hands on learning lab experience in person collaboration and direct feedback most if not all of the classes in these programs should be taught in person even for non stem programs i believe that the cap on online courses should be to percent maximum so that it creates a balance students still need to be present in the learning environment interacting with peers and faculty in real time gaining confidence communication skills and depth of understanding making more and more courses online especially foundational ones is going to devalue the degree in the long term i share this as an honest assessment you may disagree and that is understandable but this is the reality as i see it we as professors need to reflect on whether we are truly preserving the mission of education or if we are compromising it for the sake of convenience and short term enrollment gains']

Name: 20_interview_interviews_candidate_candidates
Representative_Docs: ['i m pretty sure we had a candidate using one of those ai interview assist bots during their zoom interview extremely verbose answers that were rambling sitting far from the camera and always seemed to be gazing slightly off center wild experience', 'if there is no ai in the interview then they are falling behind skip to the next interview', 'so i was recently watching a yt video about devs cheating on coding interviews that said it s estimated that nearly of developers use some kind of ai assistance to cheat on tests it sort of makes sense it s like the calculator all over again we want to gauge how well a candidate actually understands what s happening but it s also unrealistic to not let them use the tools they d be using on the job after talking to a large number of companies about their recent hiring experiences it seemed like their options were pretty limited they d either rely solely on in person interviews or they d need to change how interviews were done we decided to build a platform that lets companies design coding interviews that incorporate ai into the mix we provide two different types of interviews a web based assessment that has an llm on the left and a code editor on the right and the candidate can interact with the llm explain their approach and get guidance while coding if necessary a work trial based interview where the candidate has a set amount of time to complete the tasks that the interviewer has created the candidate is allowed to use any resources at their disposal and at the end of the interview has five minutes to upload the final code and their llm chat export for review the company can decide what tasks and questions to add to both that match what they re looking for also we d then allow the interviewer to use their discretion on whether the candidate compromised things like security code style and maintainability for shipping as well as how well they vetted the ai s responses and asked for clarification and modifications basically the idea is to mimic how the candidate would actually perform on real world tasks with the real world tools they d be using on the job we d also closely monitor the tasks and workflow of companies to ensure they re not taking advantage of candidates to get free work done and that the assessments are actually based on tasks that have already been completed by their team i don t want to drop the link here since that falls under self promotion mostly interested in understanding what your thoughts on this kind of interviewing approach']

Name: 21_resume_resumes_linkedin_cover
Representative_Docs: ['not sure how many times this has been stated but it is a full time job getting a new gig i m at job applications since may and still going getting to final rounds and interviews with ceos so an offer is close i hope a week is necessary at this point you could be doing more if needed one of my friends did close to before he found a job average time to find a new job is months my advice ai for everything companies are using it to sift through applicants you should use it as well resume co or resume io are great resources for resume refresh i paid for these and linkedin premium to utilize their ai tool to prompt gpt you also get inmail this is an extremely important tool i created a project in chat gpt i prompted it with my resume additional skills linkedin page and other experience that way every time i ask for an email for a new job it has saved memory before you apply to the black hole of a job listing screenshot the job description tailor your resume to the job description language you re getting filtered by ai on the other side use it to your advantage i literally got a no less than min from applying to one job next find the talent acquisition person for that job typically via linkedin search company people search for title talent acquisition have gpt write a humanized email expressing your work history and passion about the job you are applying to once you get an interview prompt gpt to give you questions and answers based on your experience for the job screen shot the description to remind it prompt it for questions to ask as well tell it who you are talking to and it will do a decent job adjusting questions to fit the role i m in sales so this is standard for outreach might need to tailor this approach to your position but it makes this process doable hope this helps', 'we re cooked instant rejection man that s rough i m on the other side of this i was soft money and in march doge decided my colleagues and i were parasites and cut our funding without warning i ve been looking every since i had never used any of the ais besides the nonsense google search one which is always wrong so i wrote all my cover letters tailored all of my resumes etc the old fashioned way i d say i averaged about hours per application so about apps per day working full time after two months of being ghosted with a sprinkling of rejections i took the advice of all my industry friends and started letting chatgpt help i wasn t very impressed by it the writing felt fake and sometimes it would lie about my skills but once i learned to give it better prompts and examples of my old writing it improved significantly now i can produce an acceptable cover and resume with minutes of cleaning and rewriting once it lays the groundwork so now i can send applications per day instead of now i m wondering if i m screwing myself maybe two authentic apps are better than ai written apps but everyone just ghosts you anyway so i m not even sure i m getting past the ats screening anyway it seems a bit unfair to penalize the applicant when most employers use ai to filter these applications anyway i d bet of my applications went through some ai ats that badly parsed my resume checked to see if i had the exact constellation of tech skills the job description wanted and then threw my application in the trash most of my carefully written cover letters were probably never even seen by human eyes', 'i got sick and tired of ghost jobs rd party offshore agencies on linkedin indeed so i wrote a script that fetches jobs from k company websites career pages and uses chatgpt to extract relevant information ex salary remote etc from job descriptions you can use it here hiring cafe here is a filter for software developer jobs k and counting i m also scraping every company page x day so the results will stay fresh if you check back the next day hope this tool is useful please lmk how i can improve it you can follow my progress on r hiringcafe here is my technique for doing so for scraping nerds i identified company career pages with active job listings i used the apollo io to search for companies across various industries and get their company urls to narrow these down i wrote a web crawler using node js and a combination of cheerio puppeteer depending on site complexity to find the career page of the company i discovered that i could dump the raw html and prompt chatgpt o mini to classify as a binary classification whether each page contained a job description or not i thus compiled a list of verified job page if it contains a job description or not if it contains a job description i add it to a list and proceed to step verifying legit companies this part i had to do manually but it was crucial that i exclude any recruiting firms rd party offshore agencies etc because i wanted only high quality companies directly hiring for roles at their firm i manually sorted through the company career pages this took several weeks and picked the ones that looked legit at stanford we call this technique occular regression it was doable because i only had to verify each company a single time and then i trust it moving forward removing ghost jobs i discovered that a strong predictor of if a job is a ghost job is that if it keeps being reposted i was able to identify reposting by doing a embedding text similarity search for jobs from the same company if job descriptions overlap too much i only show the date posted for the earliest listing this allowed me to weed out most ghost jobs simply by using a date filter for example excluding any jobs posted over a month ago in my anecdotal experience this means that i get a higher response rate for data science jobs compared to linkedin or indeed scraping fresh jobs x day to ensure that my database is reflective of the company career page i check each company career page x day many career pages do not have rate limits because it is in their best interest to allow web scrapers which is great for the few that do i was able to use a rotating proxy i use oxylabs for now but i ve heard good things about scraperapi crawlera building advanced nlp text filters after playing with gpt o mini api i realized i could can effectively dump raw job descriptions in html and ask it to give me back formatted information back in json ex salary yoe etc i used this technique to extract a variety of information including technical keywords job industry required licenses security clearance if the company sponsors visa etc powerful search once i had the structured json data containing salary years of experience remote status job title company name location and other relevant fields from chatgpt s extraction process i needed a robust search engine to allow users to query and filter jobs efficiently i chose elasticsearch due to its powerful full text search capabilities filtering and aggregation features my favorite feature with elasticsearch is that it allows me to do boolean queries for instance i can search for job descriptions with technical keywords of pandas or r example link here question for the community here beyond job search one thing i m really excited about this million job dataset is to be able to do a yearly or quarterly trend report for instance to look at what technical skills are growing in demand what kinds of cool job trends analyses would you do if you had access to this data']

Name: 22_jobs_labor_ubi_workers
Representative_Docs: ['so what s the name of this game reductionist teleology or total non sequitur i think you re playing fail to read for comprehension you suggested that we cannot reconcile ourselves with this technology because we do not live in a socialist utopia but which is more viable seizing the means of production or instituting a redistributionist welfare state versus suppressing superior labor saving technologies concentration of wealth digression except a concentration of wealth is not a defining feature of capitalism authoritarian regimes can and have been even more unequal than capitalistic societies you re reduced to invoking terms like neo liberal and hypercapitalist as a bumpersticker boogeymen rather than well defined arguments the scandinavian countries are in some ways more free market than the us but they re coupled with relatively robust welfare states welfare states that are so robust that the countries are mistakenly called socialist in the us taking a page from your book feel free to come up with examples of societies that have supplied all citizens with enough money to live comfortably on not on a limited experimental basis but truly universally over the long term you ve bamboozled yourself into believing your own straw man about a socialist utopia a defining feature of the human condition is scarcity poor people by today s standards would be fantastically wealthy in comparison to the vast majority of our ancestors live comfortably is relative and probably always will be given the reality of humans as social creatures striving for positional goods you ve set this goal post to obscure the fact that you re directionally weak again the welfare states of scandinavia provide generous benefits but they are not unlimited the claim in the background is that this tech will strike like an earthquake and render most human labor unnecessary except these machines and super abundance do not yet exist if the uber productive machines did exist then we can easily imagine greater material benefits reason a another absurd straw man you re comparing the tech of yesterday that has not yet replaced humans frankly it s obscene that more doctor offices have not utilized technology that was available twenty years ago no i do not want to call especially to make an appointment i will get put on hold before interrupting someone who has more important things to do let me log in to a website and select a time and reason for my meeting or better yet let my agentic ai schedule the appointment or better still i don t have to go to the office to wait in one room then wait in a smaller one for some overpaid body mechanic instead an ai equipped with cameras can diagnose me then it can order the meds then they can be delivered by a drone then i can receive an alert that they re at my doorstep the fact of the matter is that if people have the option between superior robot doctors and regular human doctors most will choose the robots not everyone of course given the option of credentialed medical doctors and snakeoil salesmen we will always have people who opt for the quacks reason b i do not entirely disagree world warping technologies even in a socialist utopia that meets your wildest material standard of living will likely neccesistate people living in community people need to feel needed but this is already a problem', 'ok so let s play this game through the sweep of human history have we changed the ways we organize our societies did we move from hunter gatherer to agricultural to feudalistic societies etc is it possible that these social changes were driven in part by emerging technologies so what s the name of this game reductionist teleology or total non sequitur is it possible that in the not so distant past we were more capitalistic than today i don t know how we re operationalizing more or less capitalistic but at the current moment in time wealth is hyperconcentrated in the uppermost stratum of society in a way that has no obvious historical precedent as of billionaires roughly of the global population control of the global wealth the most wealthy billionaires collectively control more wealth than the bottom of the world within the us the top control nearly of the country s wealth even before the covid pandemic the single greatest upward transfer of wealth in human history during which the world s ten wealthiest men doubled their net worth the wealth gap in the us surpassed that of the gilded era bear in mind that the billionaires i m talking about are the same men yes the gendered language is intentional who are pouring money into developing ai and inserting it into every facet of our lives bear in mind also that many of these people elon musk peter thiel larry ellis mark zuckerberg jeff bezos sam altman are a buddy buddy with the trump administration and or b open admirers of the unspeakably odious curtis yarvin i sincerely recommend that you read karen hao s the empire of ai which was published within the last month even conservative libertarian types like charles murray have come to terms with forms of a universal basic income taking a page from your book feel free to come up with examples of societies that have supplied all citizens with enough money to live comfortably on not on a limited experimental basis but truly universally over the long term since the number of societies matching this description is zero i m going to go ahead and say that it s better to have a world in which human beings can easily find non exploitative well paid work than is to have a world in which human beings are left scrambling for a shrinking pool of increasingly exploitative and poorly paid jobs while getting a couple thousand bucks a year to take the edge off their poverty we re not going to get any form of ubi under the trump administration but if if we ever do get ubi it s not going to be enough to live on let s operate within the confines of reality here protecting workers rights against a technology that is currently widely distrusted and disliked in fact seems like a worthwhile strategy to pursue why do you want to call a doctor s office and hear a human two reasons a i think it is vastly more likely that a human being will actually help me human beings you see generally tend to care somewhat about other human beings even if you encounter an unhelpful and unsympathetic human being if you call back later you may encounter a helpful and sympathetic human being or sometimes if your explain yourself differently the unhelpful human will turn out to be helpful after all with ai if you need anything that requires responsiveness to atypical circumstances you ll just keep running into the same brick wall over and over when you call a helpline do you find the ai assistant helpful or do you like every other human on the planet repeat representative representative i need to talk to a representative until you finally reach a fellow human b even in the unlikely scenario that advances in ai render a a non issue a society in which human human interactions have been systematically replaced by human machine interactions is fundamentally not good carriage makers are upset about automobiles a within the framework of this analogy you re a carriage maker too b that said it s a bad analogy try something along the lines of pacifists are upset about killer drones or environmentalists are upset about fracking people who value human wellbeing are upset about a technopolitical project that seems likely to diminish human wellbeing', 'many people ducuss whether premise of the question is even possible i m gonna answer the question stated if ai is ubiquitous and most career paths implode what next maybe my vision isn t the most optimistic but i ve been thinking about this long before chatgpt and current trends only seem to confirm it it all depends on the country and the individual stage the transition let s say a mega corporation based in the us or china develops the first truly general ai it becomes essential to everything coding healthcare law manufacturing creativity businesses everywhere depend on it just to stay competitive and all the money it flows back to that corporation in the country where the ai originates the government reaps massive tax revenue they can afford to roll out universal basic income ubi citizens are covered at least at a basic level life gets easier for some but for other countries they re stuck paying licensing fees for an ai they didn t build their own businesses can t compete without it and their economies hollow out jobs vanish local industries collapse ubi not sustainable for most of them they burn through reserves tensions rise some governments close their digital borders and try to build isolated systems others mimic ubi knowing it s a short term fix and some start preparing for war a new kind of military recruitment begins want a higher ubi tier join the cyber defense force maintain drones guard infrastructure enforce digital laws the line between soldier and server admin blurs eventually it erupts stage collapse and convergence war breaks out not always with bullets but through cyberattacks resource blockades digital blackouts the generation that grows up during this watches their parents die or disappear into broken systems out of the rubble comes something new a global alliance not of countries but of individuals and micro communities all connected by dependence on the mega corporation s ai citizenship isn t about geography anymore it s about access like samsung vs apple some people still rise through merit engineers artists thinkers but many rely on ubi now distributed and managed directly by the ai systems it s not freedom but it s stability countries as we know them begin to fade what remains is a corporate sovereign ai ecosystem where the rules are digital the economy is automated and human labor is optional what about the poor nations those left behind by the tech revolution rural areas developing countries are mostly ignored by the ai giants they offer little value in data or markets some resist some collapse some slowly adapt ironically these communities might remain the last places where humans still grow their own food teach their own children and make things by hand final outcome eventually quality of life improves ubi becomes more livable scarcity becomes manageable but the transition is brutal and many won t make it the ai didn t kill us we just weren t ready for what it changed yup so that s the vision lmk what you think it will certainly not happen but if it does who owns the ai matters']

Name: 24_layoffs_companies_laying_employees
Representative_Docs: ['yes surge of layoffs earlier this year had nothing to do with ai ai wasn t capable of filling productivity yet in that way what it was is that companies who overhired needed to do layoffs but ai is a way to put a positive investor spin on it not a conspiracy i think there s very obvious evidence in this klarna for example claimed they were doing layoffs because of unlocks from ai total bs the tech wasn t close enough to mature enough to enable that they just needed to do layoffs', 'i m a swe at indeed that was not affected by the layoffs here s my take there has been rounds of layoffs at indeed in the past years however this one definitely feels the most disorganized the former ceo chris hyams suddenly resigned a few weeks before the layoffs and the parent company recruit ceo deko stepped in as the new permanent ceo with a vague message of we need more ai there were an are many ai initiatives already in progress when the layoffs were announced the head of hr also put in her resignation suddenly described as a personal decision the layoffs heavily affected engineering and the vast majority of the initiatives that were previously being worked on are now on pause or totally thrown out there was discussion that the layoffs heavily targeted less senior engineers but overall the trend appears to be performance vs comp as many swe ii s were affected as well there was also discussion that many on parental leave were targeted intentionally leadership would not share the statistics on the percentage of leave takers who were affected but i ve heard anecdotes about the number being disproportionately high i have no way of knowing whether this is true there is absolutely no clarity or roadmap whatsoever besides more ai and there is an impending sense of doom among engineers most of us are actively applying for jobs elsewhere for me this is the first time since starting that i ve done this the funny thing is that indeed was always known as the landing spot for burnt out aws amazon engineers and the pitch was always bring your experience and knowledge from bigger tech companies and we ll give you a fantastic work life balance and pretty decent comp which allowed them to attract talent way above what the pay bands would suggest for example at the time i was searching for the job that i eventually got hired for i had previous adjacent tier big tech experience and final onsite interviews for two of the maang companies that i cancelled because i was so happy with indeed s pitch originally this meant you days which was the third friday of every month off as well as other cool programs such as the ability to take pay for a day workweek unrivaled parental leave even for fathers etc under chris they truly wanted to become a model employer and an example of how the future workplace could be and were always heavily profitable the calculus has changed now that they ve eliminated all of these programs and i expect that top talent is going to re evaluate the compensation vs wlb tradeoff which may result in the forced attrition that they seem to be after they are also heavily pushing productivity metrics for engineers just use more ai to code faster but as a result of the layoff re org nobody knows what they are supposed to be working on so nobody is doing anything although we were told that we will still be evaluated as usual for this performance cycle it s the nature of the industry so it doesn t bother me but it s a perfect example that in any publicly traded company the shareholders eventually get hungry for more', 'i m a google employee the layoffs have become very slow recently and almost always comes with a chance to find another team internally which you usually can as all the ai orgs are hiring like crazy also i almost see no swe layoffs it is always other job functions i think it is just easier for news agencies to report swe layoffs as that captures attention but realistically everyone is impacted i would actually say swe is probably one of the least impacted by layoffs']

Name: 25_vibe_vibe coding_coding_code
Representative_Docs: ['given this post i assume you re a proponent of vibe coding and that your comment is meant to be taken as get with the times here s the thing vibe coding is all about getting something to work and forgetting everything else which has been and always will be a bad idea vibe coding cannot fix a broken codebase nor can it ensure that the codebase remains healthy studies show that in fact codebases get worse with the introduction of ai on top of that we have all the learning lost when you don t even see the code which vibe coding encourages getting something to work is the bare minimum one can expect from a developer they re not employable if they can t get something to work so anything less is useless vibe coding is all about targeting and limiting the developer to the lowest criteria a developer can achieve', 'i ve been using these tools as coding aid too and i thought it was vibe coding but turns out that s not vibe coding computer scientist andrej karpathy a co founder of openai and former ai leader at tesla introduced the term vibe coding in february the concept refers to a coding approach that relies on llms allowing programmers to generate working code by providing natural language descriptions rather than manually writing it karpathy described it as fully giv ing in to the vibes embrac ing exponentials and forget ting that the code even exists a key part of the definition of vibe coding is that the user accepts ai generated code without fully understanding it programmer simon willison said if an llm wrote every line of your code but you ve reviewed tested and understood it all that s not vibe coding in my book that s using an llm as a typing assistant even when i use an llm as a coding assistant i still try to understand every single line of the code as much as possible and that s not vibe coding it s using an llm as a typing assistant i don t think vibe coding is a good idea that said i have a paranoid need to retain control and i can t give into the vibe', 'people will move on from vibe coding once the next big thing hits i doubt much vibe coded software makes it to market to actually get your vibe code from prompts to work together you need to understand the code enough to tweak it and at that point it s not really vibe coding is it i ve always considered vibe coding as getting ai to code for you without understanding any of the code i don t imagine vibe coders often make it to market with that approach and if they do it s a recipe for disaster']
