Name: 81_assessment_exams_exam_assessments
Representative_Docs: ['effective responses will be highly discipline specific but here are some ideas for the humanities iterative assessment in the world most writing that matters is rewriting the same should be true for writing in educational contexts having students rewrite work on the basis of feedback makes it harder to use ai for an easy pass disincentivizes using it and is better pedagogy anyway design assignments that llms will bomb it s fairly easy to write questions that someone who had attended class and participated would know to answer one way but a robot would answer a very different way an appeal to their better natures i m not so na ve enough to believe that this will work all the time but cultivating a collaborative environment and explaining how the assignments are designed to help them will convince at least some of them to give a genuine effort an appeal to self interest explaining that they ll be at a disadvantage relative to people who actually have the skills the assignments are designed to cultivate might convince one or two of them diversifying assessment presentations podcast and other non traditional forms are less susceptible to ai cheating the silver lining in the whole llm train wreck is that it does force us to revisit our assessment patterns and think carefully about what we re trying to accomplish when we set assignments my hope though not my expectation is that many places can use this opportunity to move toward a less punitive and adversarial approach to assessment', 'that seems to be what some are moving towards however i haven t used an exam in any of my classes in almost a decade as i teach in an applied learning program and exams are pretty much the antithesis of applied learning i have found that service learning is really effective and the same assignment is applicable regardless of modality i shared an example in another comment on this thread but i will share another here in one of my senior level courses i assign a term long group project focused on performing an operational analysis of a business or organization that i have predetermined the groups must collaborate to design their own group contract which details the weighting of their performance between paper and presentation their team policies for individual grading an accountability piece that they decide and any other communication policies they wish to set from there they have to visit the business conduct interviews perform both primary and secondary research break down all the operations explain them and analyze them finally they do a comprehensive swot tows on very specific om components in order to develop recommendations and implementation strategies that are delivered both to me and their targeted business i verify work has been performed by administering a survey to the business and grade their work by my standards the conclusion of the activity is a page document and a minute presentation they also review the work of another team in the class and provide feedback on what they observed about their strengths weaknesses in following the case study process and applying om recommendations they lastly conduct a peer evaluation on one another to let me know what went on behind the scenes and to make any individual grade adjustments per their contractual policies none of this requires in person attendance in my classroom and i have done this for nearly two decades now ai is actually pretty mitigated in this activity when there is verifiable work being done through required primary research and when the threat of anyone on their team using it to write their portion of the document would result in a course failure for the entire group peer pressure is a helluva drug', 'i don t know the technical reasons for why the ai sometimes can t figure out the answers to the questions it generates but i suspect it s got to do with the fact that it learns the types of questions from my slides generates new ones and then attempts to answer them in the final step rather than working backwards from the solution to the question or generating the question and answer jointly of course i can complain i ve been doing it for years but my university doesn t can t do anything about it instead they have recently advised that we need to embrace it and use it for actual learning rather than cheating hence i advised the students to understand things better using ai and generate more questions using it etc but not rely on it for answers in line with the guidance it doesn t seem to work well because despite weekly reminders they still just use it to generate answers and seem unprepared overall but without leverage complaining about it has zero effect the incentives are just stacked in the wrong way i don t admit to using it i embrace it to generate test questions as advised by my university i m not being assessed so using tools like ai is not a contravention of rules i don t need to learn the stuff i already know it so ai usage doesn t jeopardise my integrity i think you ve got this one wrong if you think other people allowing ai use makes things worse for you then maybe your assessment regime isn t robust to cheating the majority of my grades are generated by an ai proof proctored exam at the end what about yours maybe you need to work on that rather than depend on the assessment strategies of other professors outside your own institution']

Name: 82_jobs_industry_tech_graduates
Representative_Docs: ['i have sympathy for people who recently graduated from college or are about to with a cs degree and like programming even if they only picked it up during said college years they were born too late to have an easy start of their career in the industry except for lucky few at the same time i have no sympathy whatsoever for vast majority of the so called programmers i understand the economy is not a good place and even good people are being laid off nobody is safe perfectly sensible companies overhired and are now shedding their perfectly capable staff but even more so bad companies had more people hired that made any sense and most of all the people they did hire are plain incompetent i have worked in the industry for over years during which time it was almost continuously growing while experiencing constant skill shortage for political reasons companies resorted to hiring people who should not have gotten programming jobs to begin with by the rules of the game they needed a specific headcount not having the headcount makes managers vulnerable to having their budget slashed and reducing their position meaning even if the team genuinely wanted to hire someone good they were on a timer before they had to employ a bad person for political reasons and given teams notoriously struggle to find more good people most of the time they ended up with a bad one and more than at that high salaries and high suction attracted people who have no interest in programming and who should not be doing it long term they are a net loss to any project they partake in by adding technical debt solely for lacking the skills to do better not to be confused with a notion that tech debt only shows up when people are bad there are pragmatic reasons to add it vast majority of so called senior programmers never graduated past a level where they can semi fluently copy paste from stackoverflow and massage that to produce an operational crud program with bad internals this is despite several years of experience nobody knows how big of a team it makes sense to maintain and with what people instead there were just notions of maintaining a head count it was a political suicide to not do it but now with the promise of ai the climate has changed and the expected head count went down so no people are not being replaced by ai as much as they are being laid off because of the shift in what makes sense from political standpoint and most of them were not adding value anyway everybody who spent more than few months in the industry should have known the state is not sustainable', 'first off you say companies say companies still say graduates aren t job ready the result a widening skills gap that s not a widening skills gap that sounds good in an ai sales pitch but it s not widening if companies hired new graduates which by the way they do in droves because they re cheaper in this raw inexperienced form experience is more costly a skills gap is that which the student comes from university knowing and that which industry is doing there will always be a skills gap the fact that graduates aren t getting hired as you say doesn t widen a skills gap for a widening skills gap you would have to have some universities where students are getting hired and some where students are routinely not some are pulling ahead while others are not such a widening skills gap is solvable by the lagging institution recognizing they re behind and altering their curriculum to be more inline with industry demands part of the actual problem however is academia has a different value metric most faculty appointments at university require a phd and history of publications and grant funded research this does not translate into being a good effective educator having a phd also generally implies you do not have much if any work experience few employers want to hire phds because of their lack of experience and often outsized salary requirements so you end up with hyper specific specialists in one tiny niche with no experience outside academia the longer they re there the longer they re out of touch and that s a widening skills gap which is why faculty traditionally have had sabbaticals but again employers don t want you getting in their business for your own benefit with nothing to offer except haughty opinions so do faculty have first hand experience in the operations of a data center software development team or product design if not how can you expect their students to come out of school knowing any of it i work with compsci phd s at my institution that have terminal degrees but no practical experience the assumptions they make about how industry operates is almost laughable because of academic freedom they can talk a good game to the inexperienced it sounds good to non technical administrators but falls flat to practitioners because of academic organizational structure they cannot be removed for teaching poorly informed topics they have a phd after all but with nobody to hold them to account that s where the skills gap comes from', 'this is maybe the fifth time in my career i ve heard that the new normal was that developers were going to permanently be in lower demand that the industry had crashed or that the outsourcing nightmare was going to take our jobs forevermore every single time they had excuses about why this time is different tl dr high skill software engineering jobs are still hiring and will likely recover lower skill coding jobs may never be in high demand again but we have to define terms here there are coder jobs and there are software engineering jobs i m defining a coder as someone who ten years ago would have bragged about copying all of their code off of stackoverflow someone who today writes all of their code using ai and as a result they are x faster whereas a software engineer ten years ago wrote of their code from scratch and today may use ai for autocomplete or the occasional help but it frankly only provides a roughly productivity boost they re still way faster than the coder and their code is much better coders are the group having the hardest time finding jobs right now there are way way too many coders in the us for the number of coder jobs available their skills are tied to a very specific stack leaving them at the mercy of industry shifts and these are also the jobs most susceptible to outsourcing whereas the software engineers do have it rough right now but the best are still getting jobs i started job hunting in earnest in september and found a job last month i would have found one sooner if i weren t being picky about remote work and minimum salary requirements at least k but was trying for and found one over k and the above split is true for fresh graduates as well you better believe that the top graduates in terms of programming talent not grades of top universities are getting offers right out of the gate it s the lie of everyone can code that brought us here it s not true or it is but in the same sense that everyone can paint yes you can learn to use a brush yes you can make your work recognizable but not everyone will be able to get a day job as an artist no matter how much time they put in']

Name: 93_wrong_trust_ai wrong_ai correct
Representative_Docs: ['i generally cross check things ai tells me with other sources or my own understanding and i extremely rarely encounter this problem anymore on the newer advanced models while ai can still make mistakes i feel i had human teachers who taught me more bs than s ais i feel this perception of yours is largely grounded in the problems you experienced in or you re still using only free models or you work on vastly different topics than i do', 'this author gildas agbon points out how this is a consequence of the epistemic assumptions of the machine learning field the workings of the models rest on an assumption that truth is atheoretically derivable from data that isn t such a problem if your model is just reading and interpreting data and you can be sure the data itself isn t suspect but it can be a huge problem for llms because they aren t just interpreting data they re creating new texts based on it that are supposed to fit the user s purposes and if they re trained on anything and everything off the internet there s every chance what is most common in the data could be false or not fit for the user s purposes agbon calls it a hegemony of the recurrent over the true', 'you should not trust it imagine if the ai was fed only accurate information it could still misunderstand terms that mean different things in different contexts the result cannot be perfect this is the primary fundamental basis of conflict among thinking persons e g i might not have heard what you thought you said you don t need a computer to foul it up also now understand that in our world there is also misinformation both deliberate and accidental the things that you know to be true are often contradicted by other statements e g the earth if flat unfortunately the people that inject these errors are often dedicated to pushing the point here again intelligent people are often misled you don t need a computer to foul it up also']

Name: 95_calculators_calculator_math_tool

loud does this tool undermine mathematical thinking or unlock deeper understanding sound familiar back then no one knew what calculators would mean for math education there wasn t a clear plan to shift from computation to reasoning that shift happened slowly and not without conflict some skills faded and were quietly forgotten when s the last time you used a slide rule others survived because use ai it is done so very poorly as to earn scores between i thought that if they used ai less they would study and lean more nope more broadly i look at the utter sea of bullshit in which we are critical thinking skills among students', 'if you want them to think critically you will need modules on critical thinking that actually make a difference as i wrote however given that ai can already complete traditional assignments better than most people traditional pedagogy is insufficient curiously we need ai based learning potent customized and patient to teach students who can then offer value beyond what ai can optimistically we might figure out how to use ai to create educational experiences that deliver learning objectives customized to the learner if we care about critical thinking for example it will no longer suffice to claim an essay assignment does that we d want a specific critical thinking assignment in all of my courses i have critical thinking content with a set of ten exercises done throughout the semester but i ve no evidence it substantively improves critical thinking', 'the rise of generative ai genai in knowledge workflows raises questions about its impact on critical thinking skills and practices we survey knowledge workers to investigate when and how they perceive the enaction of critical thinking when using genai and when and why genai affects their effort to do so participants shared first hand examples of using genai in work tasks quantitatively when considering both task and user specific factors a user s task specific self confidence and confidence in genai are predictive of whether critical thinking is enacted and the effort of doing so in genai assisted tasks specifically higher confidence in genai is associated with less critical thinking while higher self confidence is associated with more critical thinking qualitatively genai shifts the nature of critical thinking toward information verification response integration and task stewardship our insights reveal new design challenges and opportunities for developing genai tools for knowledge work']

Name: 104_devops_web_qa_roles
Representative_Docs: ['web development is already squeezed in a few ways front end development is very popular with beginners so there are probably more people looking for entry level jobs than there are jobs for years there have been products like wordpress and wix the fact is that if you re not a programmer you can probably still make a decent website using no code tools that is a problem for the lower end of web development lots of people learn full stack development only doing front end web is basically only being able to make half a website lots of companies are not ok with that if i was recommending a year old to learn programming today i would recommend they avoided the web it s too over saturated ai is neither not a threat or a big threat depends who you listen to pick your internet stranger and listen if you want or ignore all of us probably best to ignore all of us for every wise sage like me there are are idiots here', 'the nature of which skills are valuable is going to change a lot in the next few years on the hiring side it s not clear what is needed except that the tradition roles for new hires are getting filled by ai from the academic side the schools will need to figure which skills help their graduates neither side knows what skills matter and everyone is cautious on what to change it sucks for the new graduates feeling obsolete before getting wrinkles and grey hair for context i work on embedded and scientific codes what i m seeing is changing hiring targets and essential skills fewer people are needed for implementation details more for translation of ideas into something useful in my domain that means an ability to formulate ideas into algorithms using much more math and rigor however instead of the essential skill being generation of working code it is becoming the generation of rigorous but human readable specifications i m seeing x improvements in productivity with existing tools by starting with clear algorithm definitions easier said than done and applying the ai tools to the implementation for example i was mentoring a young engineer working on kalman filters while there are good libraries to use them but some small details can require building one from scratch that was the case here using ai in less than an hour i was able to define the algorithm including visualization and data logging generate a high level description create working code and verify it worked like expected from previous experience building and debugging an implementation would have taken a day or more that is a huge change in how to work and what you need to know once things settle it will be clear how to build teams again and how junior and senior roles work together in my area at least i don t see the ai systems bridging the gap from need to implementation without assistance for a while there are too many unwritten rules and nuanced decisions what i do see is a huge need for better math and writing skills to leverage the ai systems i started professionally with assembler moved to c c then matlab simulink and python now ai looks increasingly like the next step it seems to be capable of compiling clear requirements for an algorithm defined in a human readable form into an implementation without fighting syntax and language issues because of this my hiring requirements for new hires are rapidly shifting in fact people i would not have considered hiring before because they lack implementation skills are getting a second look', 'web has been problematic for years before ai first problem almost all beginners go into web it means there are too many people applying for too few jobs second problem products like wix and other web builder apps mean that lots of people don t actually need to hire a web developer anymore the low end of web development has been squeezed for years the last time someone asked me to make a website for them i actually talked them out of it and told them to use wix they didn t need to pay me to do it the is strong demand for web developers but just far too many people applying for the jobs it doesn t matter if there are new jobs every month if people are applying for them if this is about getting a job then look at what employers are asking for near you don t pigeonhole yourself into web if employers are looking for swift or delphi or c jobs']

Name: 108_forums_discussion boards_discussion_boards
Representative_Docs: ['i hear you but if you go back to the fundamentals of neuroscience specifically how do people learn recall i think you can design classroom activities that meet what needs to be learned that field is expanding and provides a lot of guidance that is not aging out with ai regarding designing activities that interleave create emotional investment elaboration spaces out material engage purposeful practice can discussion boards always create engagement with learning modalities no could they sometimes maybe you can never just apply on pedagogical method group presentation or discussion boards and say ok all good because what is required to occur in someone s brain to complete those activities can be wildly different based on the topic the prompt etc i have wildly successful discussion boards and then awful awful dump ai slop on the screen with no discussion board it is all about how i the teacher designed the board i can manage asynchronous my line in the sand is when they say no video because the student may feel uncomfortable i have to hear and see the student in most classes like wth i will always pick that battle but yes i understand we face many challenges online but to my original point i think just saying no is a battle that will be lost', 'sure here s a well structured article on the topic education forum education forum a platform for dialogue innovation and progress in learning in an increasingly interconnected and fast changing world the importance of education cannot be overstated as societies face complex challenges ranging from technological disruption to social inequality education systems must evolve to meet new demands education forums serve as critical platforms for dialogue innovation and policy development enabling stakeholders from diverse backgrounds to collaborate on improving the quality and accessibility of education worldwide what is an education forum an education forum is typically a conference seminar or online platform where educators policymakers students researchers and community leaders come together to discuss educational issues share best practices and propose forward thinking solutions these forums may focus on specific themes such as curriculum reform digital learning teacher training education equity or early childhood development education forums can be global national or local in scope notable examples include the world education forum regional conferences like the asia pacific education forum and community level gatherings organized by local governments or ngos objectives and importance fostering dialogue and collaboration one of the primary goals of an education forum is to encourage open inclusive discussions among stakeholders teachers can share classroom experiences researchers can present data driven findings and policymakers can outline upcoming reforms this creates a feedback loop where decision making is informed by real world experiences and evidence driving innovation in teaching and learning forums often showcase innovative teaching methodologies digital tools and successful case studies these sessions help participants understand emerging trends such as artificial intelligence in classrooms gamification and blended learning models influencing policy education forums frequently produce declarations white papers or action plans that influence educational policy at various levels they provide an avenue for voices especially those from marginalized communities to be heard in policymaking processes professional development for educators forums offer valuable opportunities for continuous learning and networking workshops and panel discussions can enhance pedagogical skills and expose participants to new frameworks for have emerged as vibrant platforms where students can come together to share knowledge seek advice and foster a sense of community this article explores the significance of student forums in enhancing the academic experience and building connections among peers what is a student forum a student forum is an online platform designed for students to discuss various topics related to their education these forums can take multiple forms including discussion boards social media groups and dedicated educational platforms where students can post questions share resources and engage in conversations the primary objective is to create a supportive space for learning and collaboration benefits of student forums peer support one of the standout features of student forums is the sense of community they foster students often find themselves facing similar challenges whether it s academic pressure social issues or career choices forums provide a space for students to share their experiences and support one another helping to reduce feelings of isolation resource sharing forums are excellent for sharing academic resources study materials and recommendations for textbooks or online courses networking and collaboration forums often lead to meaningful connections among students collaborative projects can be born out of discussions and networking can enhance opportunities for internships study groups and career advice creating a positive forum environment to maximize the benefits of student forums it s essential to maintain a positive and respectful environment here are some strategies to keep discussions productive establishing guidelines clear guidelines for posting and interaction can help foster respectful communication encouraging users to be constructive and considerate can prevent negativity and trolling moderation having moderators who can guide discussions and address any inappropriate behavior ensures that the forum remains a safe space for all students encouraging inclusivity all students should feel welcome to participate promoting inclusivity by encouraging diverse voices can lead to richer discussions and more comprehensive perspectives challenges faced by student forums despite their many advantages student forums are not without challenges misinformation the open nature of forums can sometimes lead to the spread of misinformation students may encounter inaccurate information that could mislead them in their academic pursuits disengagement some forums may struggle with student participation over time leading to a lack of engagement strategies to keep discussions active and encourage contributions are critical balancing privacy and openness students may be hesitant to share personal experiences or seek advice if they fear judgment or privacy breaches balancing the openness of forums with privacy considerations is essential the future of student forums as technology continues to evolve so will the nature of student forums innovations such as artificial intelligence can help create more personalized experiences by recommending topics and connecting students with similar interests additionally integrating multimedia tools such as video discussions and live chats can enhance engagement and make forums more interactive conclusion student forums are invaluable assets in today s educational landscape they encourage collaboration knowledge exchange and community building among students as we navigate the challenges and opportunities presented by modern education fostering these online platforms can nervous about that too we have a time for questions at the end so i have to be prepared ahh has anyone dealt with stuff like this too i really really really really need and would appreciate anyone s else s experience or any tips or advice or anything here it doesn t help that i get confused on my own research lol it involves evaluating and improving forecast accuracy and i get humbled up with the forecast times stats ugh anyways i plan to read a lot of papers to hone things down in the next days and learn as much as i can about ml but i m just feeling complete terror', 'i ve seen this topic discussed a few times now in relation to instructure s recent press release about partnering with openai on a new integration i attended the instructurecon conference last week where among other things instructure gave a tech demo of this integration to a crowd of about people i don t think they ve released video of this demo publicly yet but it s not like they made us sign an nda or anything so i figured i d write up my notes i m recreating this based on hastily written notes so they may not be perfectly accurate recreations of what we were shown during the demonstrations they made it clear that these were very much still in development were not finished products and were likely to change before being released it was also a carefully controlled partially pre programmed tech demo they did disclose which parts were happening live and which parts were pre recorded or simulated in the tech demo they showed off three major examples course admin assistant this demo had a chat interface similar to every llm but its function was specifically limited to canvas functions the example they showed was typing in a prompt like emily smith has an accommodation for a two day extension on all assignments please adjust her access accordingly and the ai was able to understand the request access the assign to function of every assignment in the class and give the emily student extended access in the demo it never took any action without explicitly asking the instructor to approve the action so it gave a summary of what it proposed to do something like i see twenty five published assignments in this class that have end dates would you like me to give emily separate assign to until dates with two extra days of access in each of these assignments it s not clear what other functions the ai would have access to in a canvas course but i liked the workflow and i liked that it kept the instructor in the loop at every stage of the process the old ai sandwich principle every interaction with an ai tool should with a human and end with a human i also liked that it was not engaging with student intellectual property at any point in this process it was targeted solely at course administration settings my analysis i think this feature could be genuinely cool and useful and a great use case for ai agents in canvas streamline the administrative busywork so that the instructor can spend more time on instruction and feedback interesting promising want to see more ai assignment assistant another function was a little more iffy and again a tightly controlled demo that didn t provide many details the demo tech guy created a new blank assignment in canvas and opened an ai assistant interface within that assignment he prompted it with something like here is a pdf document of my lesson turn it into an assignment that focuses on the analysis level of bloom s taxonomy and then he uploaded his document we were not shown what the contents of the document looked like so this is very vague but it generated what looked like a competent enough analysis paper assignment one thing that i did like about this is that whenever the ai assistant generates any student facing content it surrounds it with a purple box that denotes ai generated content and that purple box doesn t go away unless and until the instructor actually interacts with that content and modifies or approves it so ai sandwich again you can t just give it a prompt and walk away the demo also showed the user asking for a grading rubric for the assignment which the ai also populated directly into the rubric tool and again every level criteria etc was highlighted in purple until the user interacted with that item my analysis this might useful in some circumstances with the right guardrails plenty of instructors are already doing things like this anyway in llms that have little to no privacy or intellectual property protections so this could be better or at least less harmful but there s a very big very scary devil in the details here and we don t have any details yet my unanswered questions about this part surrounds data and ip what was the ai trained on in order to be able to analyze and take action on a lesson document what did it do with that document as it created an assignment did that document then become part of its training data or not all unknown at this point ai conversation assignment they showed the user creating an ai conversation assignment in which the instructor set up a prompt something like you are to take on the role of the famous th century economist john keynes and have a conversation with the student about supply and demand presumably you could give it a lot of specific guidance on how the ai is to guide and respond to the conversation but they didn t show much detail then they showed a sequence of a student interacting with the ai keynes inside of an llm chat interface within a canvas assignment it showed the student trying to just game the ai and ask for the answer to the fundamental question and the ai told it that the goal was learning not getting the answer or something like that of course there s nothing here that would stop a student from just copying and pasting the canvas ai conversation into a different ai tool and pasting the response back into canvas then it s just ai talking to ai and nothing worthwhile is being accomplished then the part that i disliked the most was that it showed the instructor speedgrader view of this conversation assignment which showed a weird speedometer interface showing how engaged the student was in the conversation it did allow the instructor to view the entire conversation transcript but that was hidden underneath another button grossest of all it gave the instructor the option of asking for the ai s suggested grade and written feedback for the assignment again ai output was purple and wanted instructor refinement but gross my analysis this example i think was pure fluff and hype the worst impulses of ai boosterism it wasn t doing anything that you can t already do in copilot or chatgpt with a sufficient starting prompt it paid lip service to academic integrity but didn t show any actual integrity guardrails the amount of ai agency being used was gross the faith it put in the ai s ability to actually generate accurate information without oversight is negligent i think there s a good chance that this particular function is either going to never see the light of day or is going to be very different after it goes through some refinement and feedback processes']

Name: 115_ethical_ethical use_ethics_responsible
Representative_Docs: ['does your institution actually embrace responsible use of ai or does it just not ban ai use the two are very different and there are several resources available on responsible ai use and training students and faculty about the pros and cons of various uses of ais from your title i was hoping there was some introduction to what ai can and cannot do well and how it can both hinder and help in various tasks as well as some ethics modules and bias awareness from your post though it looks like your university just says you can use it i would encourage you to look into the resources available i found that incorporating ai ethical guidelines into about one lecture s worth of time spread over three time periods actually lead to more students being more responsible there will always be some using any tool as a crutch but your more motivated students can be trained to learn even in an age of easy distraction', 'our university provided no such guidelines ugh i don t have time now but there are several organizations that actually teach about responsible ai us check out the montreal declaration and look for the mozilla foundation on ai use ideally rather than some vague guidelines buried in a syllabus or the like students will receive a little bit of training in their university orientation and some continued training in some ge courses to reinforce the ethics part this is new enough technology that very few secondary teach about it so we need to pick up the slack as for alphafold while not the end all be all it or another similar ai has solved the decades old prediction of protein structure from primary sequence something i worked on many many years ago ais can be amazing tools if used by people who understand what they are doing they can also provide seemingly plausible answers for people who don t know what they are doing it s key that students understand they don t think they don t reason they don t feel pity or feel insert terminator gif here but they can predict they can be an embodiment of statistical probability but just like those old models i used to predict protein structure sometimes they can go haywire and blow up a prediction to ludicrous results if the students don t know enough to double check then they are worthless in their field emphasize that students can be replaced if they cannot reason i m fortunate now to be in a much more selective institution where the students know they need to know a tremendous amount of information ai can help them collate the data they need to know can suggest a schedule to optimize their studying can help create different types of questions and case studies to enhance their studying and a lot more but the students still have to learn the material we have an amazing secure testing center too plus a strong ethics training with potentially sever consequences that have happened so ai use on exams is not an occurrence even still some students rely a little too much on finding shortcuts which hurts them when it comes exam time fortunately we provide resources to help them figure out how to study and learn and most mature into good students i think regular reinforcement of responsible use helps keep most students on track just like all those boring safety trainings we ve had to go through it is now second nature to be safe in lab', 'yes our institution embraces the responsible use of ai at least that is what they keep talking about we have examples of potentially good uses of ai in delivering education such as creating virtual avatars for the students to practice their bed side manners we also hold competitions where students pitch their ideas on how ai can be incorporated into biomedical sciences ironically several of the proposed ideas seem to be ai generated from your title i was hoping there was some introduction to what ai can and cannot do well and how it can both hinder and help in various tasks as well as some ethics modules and bias awareness what ai can do is just dependent on what is in the training data what ai does not do well are on tasks that require knowledge that is too different from the data in the training set in general ai based on deep neural networks would be good at tasks that involve human perception such as visual and auditory perceptions neural networks were in fact inspired by how neurons work and convolutionary neural networks were inspired by visual receptive fields so these tasks include things like image and speech recognition with llms ai can emulate the writing available on the internet so llms can be trained to sound more empathetic more ethical and more polite than the average human llms are terribly at reasoning because they were never trained to do reasoning until late in the training process e g fine tuning to solve competition and exam questions llms will do well on questions that are similar to questions in the training data and terribly on questions that are dissimilar to questions in the training data but my concern is that the pervasive use of ai by the students during learning will deprive the students of the opportunity to learn basic technical skills that are important for building the foundation to solve more difficult tasks or even to recognize when llms are outputting nonsense i found that incorporating ai ethical guidelines into about one lecture s worth of time spread over three time periods actually lead to more students being more responsible our university provided no such guidelines things are in flux there is a new course that teaches the responsible use of ai as far as i can tell this course just helps the students avoid plagiarism i think the instructors for that course are from humanities so they would ask the students to paraphrase the outputs in my biology course it is evident that the humans have taken this course on using ai because they paraphrased the ai generated answers even though these answers are wrong i had accidentially created a situation where i know which students were paraphrasing ai outputs mindlessly essentially i asked the same question twice the second time i asked the question i asked the students to provide a specific citation many students who answered the second question correctly did not go back and fix their answer to the first question last week we had a workshop on ai ethics and this just turned out to be a ai hype train the first faculty member writes books on how ai will revolutionize the world and he is confident that alphafold has made protein structure experiments obsolete this is not true because we still need to solve protein sturctures for new proteins that are highly dissimilars from proteins whose structures have been solved protein structures also need to solved for protein protein interactions there will always be some using any tool as a crutch but your more motivated students can be trained to learn even in an age of easy distraction this was my hope intially i didn t bother to make my assignments ai proof initially i didn t realize that things could get so bad that the students who i thought were motivated are also relying on ai without thinking about the ai generated answers']

Name: 117_education_difficulties_friction_basic skills
Representative_Docs: ['it s worth pointing out that the problem isn t ai itself it s how we choose to integrate it into the classroom right now many schools are still treating ai like some sudden uncontrollable force instead of treating it like a tool that can be managed just like calculators phones or even google itself when it first became widespread there are simple ways to reduce students misusing ai make more of the work classroom based and discussion heavy have students explain their thinking verbally or in writing require handwritten drafts or in class brainstorming before allowing typed work create assignments that ai can t easily complete personal connections classroom specific references critical thinking questions also i think it is essential that we teach students how to use ai responsibly most adults i know use it for lesson planning writing and editing emails reports resumes coding help and debugging language translating etc etc i don t think we re heading toward total brain mush dystopia i think we re facing a challenge that schools and educators can meet if we start adapting we should be teaching how to use ai as a tool it isn t going to disappear', 'ai isn t what s get the hate it s the self promoting developers that have very little concept of what goes on in a classroom or any idea of what schools need it s almost all imagined and never informed by actual schools just because you went to school once upon a time doesn t mean you understand how schools operate that s like saying you flew by airplane once and now you re a pilot or you ate at a restaurant and now you re a chef basic business sense says to understand the industry you re working in then find solutions to common problems all the self promoting slop we ve seen is just imagining what the problems are based on the developers desparate grasp at what education really needs just the other day some slop slinger said they had meetings lined up with schools but decided now is a good time to figure out compliance i d wager to bet the vast vast majority of these ai hacks that vibe coded their whole offering started on the premise that ai is the solution and then went in search of a problem nobody goes to the hardware store finds a cool looking tool like some demolition sledge hammer buys it then goes home and looks for how to use it it s a sledge hammer so naturally i need to find a wall to tear down nevermind that the wall was never intended to be removed is potentially load bearing and the house still has a leaking roof and unfinished exterior but yeah you have fun with your shiny new tool without ever considering the need first and finding the right tool for the job second i used to tell developers slinging uninformed apps to seek out classroom teachers first or better still become a teacher and discover needs through lived experience but we all know that would never happen it s too much work it s far easier to just delete their posts based on set rules against self promotion astroturfing or feeling out the market', 'i don t have time right now to read every single reply or to even go into the full depth of my counter ai arguments for high school kids but i m going to give the top line issue i take with most of the feedback here and the main thing that i express to my own high school students i m not a big fan of ai and i don t use it much if at all there are lots of reasons for that but i won t get into them here i abhor it for high school students frankly for college students as well but i would be a tiny bit more flexible there among the many issues with the philosophy people are adopting around ai are the following kids need to learn how to use it no they don t not yet anyway what kids need to learn are basic skills without those basic skills they can t learn to use ai intelligently because they are not intelligent enough to understand and evaluate when it is working properly and when it is not at this point in their lives they should be building those basic skills they need to do things the hard way in order to learn to think critically failure to do that means they will simply accept what they see unchallenged whether it s right or wrong because they don t know what s right or wrong high school and arguably college should be about building enough basic skills to be able to engage with these tools effectively for my part i think we ve reached the stage of technological evolution where i may become a bit of a philistine i have accepted that i m prepared to be mocked for it secondly and probably lastly for now generative ai goes too far in removing the friction from life i firmly believe this we do not grow we do not improve without friction if i use the right tools i can bench press lb but if the tools i m using or offsetting of those pounds i m not benefiting from that at all that s what ai is doing right now for students they think it sounds smart some of them might even be diluting themselves into thinking that they re using it to learn how to sound smarter they are not and i know they are not because they don t know enough to know how to learn from it see my previous point we have spent our entire existence as a species looking for ways to reduce friction to increase efficiency and survival that has largely been an important pursuit but we are about to cross the rubicon in this regard and our efforts to reduce friction have already become deleterious those of us especially the young ones who are relying on ai heavily are being weakened intellectually and emotionally by it we re not challenged to improve and get better we don t have to deal with the disappointment of failure these students relying too heavily on ai are not learning a lot of important intellectual emotional and psychological skills actually i remembered one last point part of the problem is cultural especially with regards to higher education everybody is looking at higher education through the lens of a cost benefit analysis if the cost of higher education cannot be quantifiably proven to be beneficial then it s not worth it i ll begin this claim by acknowledging that higher education is simply too expensive now when i was in college in the late s and early s i was paying between and a semester tuition only at that price i could absolutely afford to go to school for personal edification and that is my point there s a great deal to college beyond just the skills it teaches you skills i would argue you are not learning if you are simply using chat gpt by the way there s the personal development there is the aforementioned learning to deal with difficulties and challenges how do you cope with having too much work and not enough time how do you deal with critical feedback that you feel was unfair how do you grow and improve when facing these challenges how do you meet the expectations of your professors especially when they appear to contradict each other the use of generative ai does not teach us how to do these things it teaches us how to avoid them so of course if you re not going to school to learn any of these skills and don t recognize their value in your life it may seem pointless to do with the old fashioned way but it s literally a case of the old adage you don t know what you re missing by robbing yourself of all of these challenges difficulties and frictions you are robbing yourself of tremendous growth opportunities but since all you want to do is avoid the difficulties you have no consciousness or awareness of the benefits gained by going through them the earlier we start letting our children avoid those difficulties with the use of ai the harder it will be to reverse these challenges there s more i could say but that s all i have time for at the moment i m fully prepared for people to come in and roast me over this i will probably ignore most of you because i just don t have the time or energy for it i am not completely closed to being persuaded about some of the virtues of ai but i am pretty close to closed regarding the virtues of ai for people under or maybe somebody has a good argument but i have yet to hear one']

Name: 119_grading_feedback_exams_detailed feedback
Representative_Docs: ['i m working on a project to build an ai powered auto grading system for math assignments and exams and i d love to get some input the goal is to help save time on grading while still giving students meaningful feedback it s not about replacing human grading tas or professors would still validate the grades but we re hoping it can make the process a bit smoother this idea came from a professor who told us how grading so many exams and homeworks was overwhelming to manage they came up with a policy they d randomly pick a few questions to grade for accuracy and the rest were just marked for completion it totally makes sense given the workload but the downside is that students don t get full feedback on how they did this can leave them in the dark about what they need to work on especially before exams that s where we re hoping this system could help it would grade all questions provide detailed feedback and save a lot of time but here s the thing we don t know how academic criteria for grading are usually defined are rubrics common for most assignments and exams or is it more about professional judgment as you go we want to make this tool actually useful for professors and tas not something that feels like extra work or a bad replacement for human grading if you have any advice on how you approach grading or how we could make this better i d love to hear Name: 120_cs_grads_degree_cs degree
Representative_Docs: ['i mean with a truly rigorous cs degree where you built a deep mathematical foundation and went into formal methods etc do you think many cs grads go through rigorous degree programs hell even for those that do do you think they retain this knowledge ai literally frees you from all the boring work that even many bootcamp grads could do while true i d say most swe jobs are crud web app or basic plugging shit into other shit work even in big tech many teams are working on slightly fancier crud now you are free to let all this boring work be done by ai and instead focus on posing the truly transformative questions based on your deep knowledge gained at university ai just isn t there yet bluntly and i say this as someone that spent the better part of this decade working on ai problems in big tech it won t get there for a while besides graduates arguably aren t at that level yet if they re not going into research they re probably not working on cs problems of this sub is about software engineering not cs like the only viable reasoning would be that your university jumped on the hype train and designed their cs curriculum to teach you what was relevant at the time instead of focusing on the rigorous and timeless computer science foundations i think you re overselling an undergraduate cs degree but you re probably right it s sadly the nature of cs even at good universities to cater for industry instead of academia', 'just wanted to ramble a bit here but it seems like cs is loosing it s clout and the terrible employment market for cs is finally getting recognition after years of being ignored for most new grads it s too little too late the job market has been effectively dead since early case in point the unemployment for cs and for ce was numbers i expect the numbers released for next year to be far far worse and the numbers in will be interesting anyways cs seems to be loosing it s clout and social value on one hand i am happy this major is getting exposed for the bs hype but on the other hand i am not sure if i want my educational credentials to be considered useless especially compared to a psychology or philosophy major exhibit a economic nihilism online communities gamer culture w brandon atrioc ewing basically in this podcast the yt er atrioc talks about how his friend is unemployed with a cs degree from berkeley not just to anyone but on gavin newsom s podcast this is definitely going to hit normie middle aged folk or older in reply newsom says that the degree once thought to be useful might be less useful than philosophy this isn t exactly what he says but this is what i understood from the convo you can see for yourself apparently philosophy is more useful for today s job market than cs not sure how accurate that is either way this is very dangerous to the reputation of cs degree holders imho while cs degrees aren t the hardest degree to get they are definitely up there in difficulty atleast at reputable universities i feel like in the internet culture this podcast might reach exhibit b big tech told kids to code the jobs didn t follow a nyt podcast with a whole topic focused on the demise of cs grads it s fairly well done though a couple years too late a kid named nate talks about how architecture might be a better career than cs frankly i never heard a good thing about architecture so to hear this is saddening it also goes over the history of the cs hype starting in which i liked but with the advent of ai it seems is being completely abandoned and the entire focus is on developing ai skills from big tech instead of coding which i don t even know what ai skills are sending prompts to gpt is a skill idk overall it s a very good takedown on the hype of cs from the big tech perspective cs is definitely getting a negative hit in culture with those with the degree left holding the bag what do you guys think cs while seemingly relevant to the times is also the most useless precieved it doesn t make sense meanwhile the useless degrees have lower unemployment and are being praised by ai companies', 'everyone complains about the job market on this subreddit this is nothing new and the job market will continue to get worse for us as corps and ceos require us to jump through more and more hoops for worse and worse jobs while this may already be something most people here passively agree with we should actively seek to propagate two facts that the cs job market for new grads especially is cooked there are no more easy to get jobs that if you do manage to get a career in this field the work life balance is awful and the pay is beginning to stagnate there are no more lucrative comfy jobs why do this why shit talk our field even exaggerate a touch about how bad it is again two reasons it is simply true that the new grad market is legitimately a joke while the overall unemployment rate for new grads of any major is already higher than the national average of the average unemployment rate for new cs grads is a whopping there is also evidence that of all cs degree holders are under employed doing jobs that don t require a cs degree it stands to reason the under employment rate for new grads is even higher simply put statistical evidence shows that cs is not a field to choose if you are looking for easy guaranteed employment after university even if it is not as bad as we say it is it is in our own interest to make cs seem like an unappealing field and generally discourage others from joining this may sound underhanded but at this point it is justified for a long time cs was sold by bootcamps day in the life videos ceos and even mainstream politicians as the future the path forward that it was this cool new field where training required was minimal bachelor s degree at best the pay was outstanding the work life balance and company culture was great playground themed offices and wearing a hoodie to work anyone and the work was interesting and above all impactful and important for a while this was true but at this point this rhetoric is a trick a lie purposefully propagated to ensure there is an oversupply of workers that can be leveraged to suppress wages degrade working conditions and exploit developers those at the top have lied and continue to lie about the opportunities in this field for their own gain if we want to gain an advantage in this labor market we must hit them where it hurts ultimately i think many of you know something has soured in this industry that something is going or has gone wrong and you are right the oversupply of new grads is one dimension but ai h bs layoff culture etc have all worked in tandem to destroy this profession however there isn t really much we can do about those other factors when it comes to discouraging people who might major in cs it is the best decision college freshman who might have joined cs will just join another major more informed about what the cs field has to offer they will be steered away to purse another hopefully successful career no harm no foul and students who are really passionate will still join in sizable numbers new grads won t be cut to zero but in time the supply of workers will dry up and employers won t be able to treat us like shit anymore so don t be passive or reserved about what the cs market is like whenever you can bring it up make a comment talk about it until its a little annoying post about it online if a family member heading to uni asks you about it tell them the truth disagree publicly with people who are promoting cs as a solid stable career choice heck even make jokes about cs grads being homeless every little bit helps it doesn t take much effort do whatever you can and refuse to let more and more young people be lured into this trap so that they can be exploited and preyed upon by large corporations and ceos']

Name: 121_skill_ai skill_skill using_knowing
Representative_Docs: ['can you walk me through what help make the most of my brain means with ai i truly don t get it or see it what i see is a potential better interface for the internet cell phone instead of having to text a friend while driving a better interface for hearing what i said and actually texting that instead of some nonsense or when i m driving and want to hear a song actually playing that song instead of calling my ex', 'ai is a drug you shouldn t take if ai is a drug for you then you shouldn t take it you need to first understand if using an ai is working like a drug for you if you re learning real skills using it then ok if not then try other sources varies from person to person not everyone takes things the same way', 'what the fuck does everyone mean when they say knowing how to use ai it s like any other tool so things like understanding what it s likely to be successful with and what it isn t structuring a prompt to maximize success understanding context and giving it the right amount of it knowing when to stop using it knowing not to trust its output etc a lot of people don t know that stuff and end up wasting more time than they needed to or they lose time by not using it at all when it would ve been helpful based on the handful of shitty experiences they had']

Name: 122_boyfriend_experience_job_uni
Representative_Docs: ['i m a senior engineer yoe and a friend of the family s kid is a recent grad who naturally hasn t been able to land a job given he didn t do any internships before graduating his dad asked me if there was anything i could do and i cringed but told him to have the kid text me and i d see what i could do to get him on track but not to expect miracles my plan for advising him is to set expectations not having any experience on his resume exiting college will make it impossible to land any engineering role in the current job market he probably needs to run a year long marathon to get where he wants to be i think his only shot is going to be a tech adjacent role probably helpdesk or support but maybe a business analyst or junior product project manager role if he is incredibly lucky of course he needs to continue applying to junior engineer openings on the off chance they give him an interview for the experience of interviewing alone even making these concessions he needs to wrap his mind around months to a year of applying everyday with nothing to show for it if his parents need him to contribute to the household a mcjob will be necessary to make ends meet while applying i d have him come up with a product it doesn t have to be groundbreaking disruptive profitable or even revenue generating what s important is that he build it from zero and that the result is usable by some audience to build the product i d advise him to leverage ai the same way we used to leverage stackoverflow once he ships an mvp he can include it on his resume but he needs to continue to iterate on it if he lands a eng adjacent role he needs to stay there for years at least to avoid being labeled as a job hopper keep working on the side project or kick off a new one automate tasks at work with and without ai did i miss anything or is anything above way off base i don t want to scare the kid but at the same time this market is horrendous for seniors i can t imagine having nothing but coursework on my resume right now', 'years of experience professionally years as a project program manager and thought leader this doesn t count as dev experience and if this is the latest experience he has it means his experience is years out of date the difference between s and in terms of way we do things is pretty significantly different s cloud wasn t even really taking off and even languages look drastically different today years experience ai ml scientist at ibm watson great experience for ai ml jobs not sure if that s what he s specifically looking for also depends on what he did specifically on the ibm watson team it might actually pigeon hole him into very specific niche things where the only other companies doing that sorta thing would be places like openai which have a lot of competition might not open doors in smaller companies if it s too niche certification publications bachelors os game engine scripting language generally won t be relevant to anyone that has long term work experience like this guy unless the publication is specifically for the role he s applying for a large amount of his development experience also won t be relevant to today either because he s been working for such a long time years ago is i doubt any employer really cares about his experience from in this market as it probably isn t that relevant like there s Name: 124_professors_profs_comment_professor
Representative_Docs: ['it s stem it seems pretty split among the stem professors on my campus tbh i have also had some business professors who hate ai too but that s not my focus i m taking a class right now that is actually required the use of ai for some of our assignments which is really weird i personally don t love the huge push to use ai and think we need to have more discussions on responsible and ethical use of ai it also feels wasteful to use it so heavily for no reason we need to have open discussions with students about why using ai is harmful and how to mitigate those harms', 'i find it so interesting that some professors hate ai while others love it some professors i ve had ban any use of it and condemn it i have had multiple professors pushing the use of ai including my pi who when i try to do something myself and it s taking a while he says just ask chatgpt or gemini use all the tools at your disposal to get things done more efficiently i m over here trying to learn things on my own i think at this point don t hold it against them they could have written something themselves then asked chatgpt or gemini to edit it for them this is something that is being pushed on students as a tool they should be using now at least in my experience i do think if we are going to be pushing it this hard though we should be teaching ethical responsible use of ai', 'the reason i asked is because the original comment i responded to said that there are some professors who say oh yeah it is ok to use ai for this and that the way it was phrased was a comment directly criticizing fellow professors who allow ai use in any way so i was trying to gauge that individual s opinion on whether they felt there were any acceptable uses of ai in education at all or if it is blanket no the original comment made it sound like any professor who does is a disgrace to the profession and should be ashamed for allowing its use i am not ignorant to what student s are using ai for i am well aware that it has made cheating more rampant and is hurting students who use it irresponsibly in the long run but it is naive for anyone to think it is going away and to try to fight it completely for better or for worse this is just a product of times changing and educators need to either adapt to these changes or fall behind the times it s no different than when any new technology is developed and productized people complain and fight it but those who fail to adapt fall behind']

Name: 125_group_told_class_sex
Representative_Docs: ['last semester i had a student john in a general biology lab section from the beginning he would do as little work as possible there is a project to research a topic of their choice design a simple experiment carry it out and collect the data and write a report they are to include evidence that they carried out the experiment photos surveys etc when he turned it in at a glance it was extremely brief and his evidence was a just list of names of people who were the subjects i sent him a message that we needed to talk about the evidence he supplied he came to office hours immediately and was defensive and combative he said doesn t have time for this that others have used ai for their report i asked if he had any other evidence and he provided me with a grocery receipt while he was there another student came in to tell me that she forgot to include her survey instrument with her evidence and i told her to email it to me she had already included other evidence including data from each subject he raised his voice claiming that she was being treated differently he used profanity said that he wasn t an yo that i could push around he s doesn t even need the class has a business and could not work for years you get the picture he demanded to know the grade on his project report and i told him they weren t graded yet he asked what i thought about what he turned in and i told him it looked brief but i reiterated that it had not been graded only skimmed ff to the next week the lab included differences of sex development there was data about testosterone levels in a large number of males and females questions about if hormone levels are an accurate way to determine sex sex is more complicated than xx or xy etc while we were discussing this he verbalized that he disagreed with the data he couldn t explain his reasoning he just disagreed i redirected back to the given data and he dismissed it we moved on after lab he came to office hours again first he apologized for his behavior the week before then said what is your thought process and asked what me meant about me was his reply then he went on to say that he s dealt with people like me before that i m harassing him that i m very political all kinds of vitriol i asked him what he was hoping to accomplish through this meeting he couldn t answer but raised his voice said that i give preference to other students in the class etc as he continued to go on i told him this was not productive and he was free to leave he did i immediately emailed my department chair about the interaction as i was considering reporting the student s behavior the next morning i reported the students behavior he was notified of the discipline report and that he had a discipline conference a few days later another student stayed after lab to tell me that he was trying to rally other students to file a complaint against me it ends up that day after he received notice he filed a complaint with hr for gender discrimination to me this seems to be retaliation i was notified that they are required to do an investigation and that i will be interviewed in the meantime he failed to show up Name: 126_letter_recommendation_write letter_letters
Representative_Docs: ['there is no way an ai letter of recommendation is at all useful for admissions', 'the fact that if you re writing a recommendation letter you should be arsed to spend an hour of your time doing it if you can t do that you shouldn t be writing a letter if the raw writing is a bottleneck that s an indication you re a shit writer if you can t do this it s an indication you re say yes to too many people only say yes to people for whom you can write serious letters that is your own prose also small point some students may not want you to be feeding their cv even bullet points into an llm nope there s not really any time savings even if there is it s not your voice you re not writing the letter you re letting chatgpt do the writing if i ever noticed a faculty member doing this my opinion of the would plummet real fast if they re willing to farm out the most personal task a recommendation letter to chatgpt they re the kind of person who s letting someone else write their words for them', 'your professor likely asked you to write a draft because they might not know you very well so it would be better for you to write about your own accomplishments so that they can look it over and then either sign it or put it in their own words you can use chat gpt to get an idea of the format of a recommendation letter and use that as a guide but you should write it yourself if you can t care enough about yourself to write the letter why should your professor care and giving them a draft that looks like it was written by ai might turn them off of doing a letter of recommendation for you']
